summary: Gruber follows up on his Ninjawords App Store censorship story after receiving a detailed on-the-record email from Apple SVP Phil Schiller. Schiller argues that Apple never asked Ninjawords to censor its dictionary — they wanted a 17+ parental control rating, which wasn't yet available when the app was first submitted. Matchstick Software chose to filter words themselves to ship sooner rather than wait for iPhone OS 3.0's parental controls. Gruber accepts this explanation but pivots to the larger issue: App Store ratings are applied inconsistently, with other dictionary apps containing the same words rated as low as 4+. He argues the real problem is the lack of a meaningful appeals process for developers. Gruber sees Schiller's willingness to respond publicly and in detail as the first real sign that Apple's leadership is trying to course-correct the App Store review process.
key_points: Apple did not demand Ninjawords censor its dictionary — the developer chose to filter words to ship before parental controls launched with iPhone OS 3.0
The timing gap between Ninjawords' May submission and iPhone OS 3.0's June release created a situation where 17+ ratings weren't yet available
Other dictionary apps containing identical 'objectionable' words carry ratings as low as 4+, exposing inconsistent enforcement by App Store reviewers
The fundamental problem isn't individual reviewer mistakes but the absence of a meaningful appeals process for developers
Schiller's detailed, on-the-record response represents the first concrete evidence that Apple's leadership is attempting to fix App Store review dysfunction
money_quotes: Apple did not censor the content in this developer's application and Apple did not reject this developer's application for including references to common swear words.
17+ ratings were not available when we launched, which means at that time, it was simply not possible for our dictionary to be on the App Store without being censored.
Apple may slap a 17+ rating on our app and wash their hands, saying 'you're not required to censor your app', but at the same time, they're putting a great deal of pressure on us to do so.
Who wants to be the only illicit dictionary on the App Store?

I believe Phil Schiller that Apple's policy is not to reject App Store dictionaries for containing swear words. However, it's clear this policy has not been consistently enforced by the App Store review team.
The problem is that there's no good recourse for developers to appeal such a mistake.

That Schiller was willing to respond in such detail and length, on the record, is the first proof I've seen that Apple's leadership is trying to make the course correction that many of us see as necessary for the long-term success of the platform.
key_insight: The App Store's problem isn't bad policy but inconsistent enforcement and the lack of a developer appeals process, and Schiller's public engagement is the first real sign Apple's leadership recognizes this.
